name: Bees, butterflies, bugs & birds
description: Insects are integral to a balanced ecosystem in gardens or parks. We can encourage birds, bats, and beneficial insects by protecting or providing nesting sites and refuges, as seen along this trail.
cemeteryDescription:
**Fulham Cemetery** was established in 1865 and is the oldest of the parish's cemeteries. It was designed by the eminent Victorian architect Sir Arthur Blomfield (1829-1899), son of the Bishop of London. The burial ground was extended in 1874 and 1880.

**Fulham Cemetery Friends** 
Fulham Cemetery Friends was set up in 2024 by local residents to help care for Fulham Cemetery – its memorials, trees, and wildlife. We aim to improve biodiversity in the cemetery and educate the public about the cemetery’s history and ecology.

[www.fulhamcemeteryfriends.org.uk](https://www.fulhamcemeteryfriends.org.uk)

waypoints.title: Bat boxes
waypoints.description: The places that bats choose  to roost, like holes in trees,  old tunnels and nooks and  crannies in buildings, are  becoming increasingly scarce.  Bat boxes can help bats by  giving them somewhere new to roost or can replace an existing roost.

waypoints.title: Northwest meadow
waypoints.description: The unmowed area north of the chapel has lower soil fertility and high density of graves, resulting in a high wildflower diversity. An excellent spot to observe butterflies and bees from June to September!

waypoints.title: Bird boxes
waypoints.description: Natural nest sites for birds in trees and old buildings are declining, especially in cities. We can provide new places for birds to nest by putting up nest boxes.

waypoints.title: Bee & insect box
waypoints.description: The National Trust Apex Bee & Insect House is beautifully designed to  offer shelter to a variety of beneficial insects. For example, solitary bees (a non-stinging species) like to use the holes to lay their eggs in.

waypoints.title: Butterfly & bee box
waypoints.description: These twin-habitat nest boxes are designed to accommodate butterflies, solitary bees, and wasps, helping to pollinate plants and trees.

waypoints.title: Log pile
waypoints.description: Log piles create a village for all things creepy and crawly. In turn, this busy community will attract birds, hedgehogs and frogs looking to snack on a tasty morsel.

waypoints.title: South and southeast meadows
waypoints.description: The large southeast meadows offers lots of open grassland and less tree cover, preferred by many butterflies and other flying insects, and also bats. You'll spot lots of bees and butterflies from May to September!

waypoints.title: Dead tree trunks
waypoints.description: When trees die, rather than cutting it down, it’s better to leave the trunk to be gradually broken down by fungi, providing habitat for beetles and other insects. Birds such as woodpeckers use them to find insects to eat, others, such as tits, nest in hollow logs.

waypoints.title: Wildflower areas
waypoints.description: Fulham Cemetery Friends sowed these areas with 2 different native wildflower seed mixes, sustainably harvested in the UK. We are trying to increase the diversity of wildflowers to attract more pollinators. The flowers are also spreading outside the areas!
